name: Tafsir al-Mizan
author: Allamah Muhammad Husayn Tabatabai
text:
# Ayah

```arabic
۞ يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ كُونُوا۟ قَوَّٰمِينَ بِٱلْقِسْطِ شُهَدَآءَ لِلَّهِ وَلَوْ عَلَىٰٓ أَنفُسِكُمْ أَوِ ٱلْوَٰلِدَيْنِ وَٱلْأَقْرَبِينَ ۚ إِن يَكُنْ غَنِيًّا أَوْ فَقِيرًا فَٱللَّهُ أَوْلَىٰ بِهِمَا ۖ فَلَا تَتَّبِعُوا۟ ٱلْهَوَىٰٓ أَن تَعْدِلُوا۟ ۚ وَإِن تَلْوُۥٓا۟ أَوْ تُعْرِضُوا۟ فَإِنَّ ٱللَّهَ كَانَ بِمَا تَعْمَلُونَ خَبِيرًا (١٣٥)
```

```english
O you who have faith! Be maintainers of justice and witnesses for the sake of Allah, even if it should be against yourselves or [your] parents and near relatives, and whether it be [someone] rich or poor, for Allah has a greater right over them. So do not follow [your] desires, lest you should be unfair, and if you distort [the testimony] or disregard [it], Allah is indeed well aware of what you do. (135)
```

## (Commentary)

His saying, the Exalted: **يا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا كُونُوا قَوَّامِينَ بِالْقِسْطِ شُهَداءَ لِلَّهِ** "O you who have faith! Be maintainers of justice and witnesses for the sake of Allah" (4:135). Justice (القسط) is equity (العدل), and maintaining justice (القيام بالقسط) means acting according to it and being vigilant in its preservation. Thus, "those who maintain justice" (القوامين بالقسط) refers to those who uphold it in the most complete and perfect manner, without swerving or deviating from it toward its opposite due to any factor such as whim (الهوى), emotion, fear, greed, or anything else.

This quality is the closest factor and the most complete means for following the truth (الحق) and preserving it from being lost. Among its branches is the necessity of truthfulness in bearing and delivering testimony (الشهادة).

From this, it becomes clear that beginning with this quality in this verse—which is directed toward explaining the ruling of testimony—and then mentioning the quality of testimony itself, is a form of progression from a general description to one of its derivatives. It is as if it were said: "Be witnesses for the sake of Allah; yet this will not be possible for you until you are maintainers of justice; so be maintainers of justice so that you may be witnesses for the sake of Allah."

His saying, **شُهَداءَ لِلَّهِ** "witnesses for the sake of Allah," the preposition "for" (اللام) denotes purpose; that is, be witnesses such that your testimony is for Allah, as He, the Exalted, said: **وَأَقِيمُوا الشَّهادَةَ لِلَّهِ** "and maintain the testimony for the sake of Allah" (65:2). The meaning of the testimony being "for Allah" is that it be in adherence to the truth and for the sake of manifesting and reviving it, as clarified by His saying: **فَلا تَتَّبِعُوا الْهَوى أَنْ تَعْدِلُوا** "so do not follow [your] desires, lest you should swerve" (4:135).

His saying, the Exalted: **وَلَوْ عَلى أَنْفُسِكُمْ أَوِ الْوالِدَيْنِ وَالْأَقْرَبِينَ** "even if it should be against yourselves or [your] parents and near relatives" (4:135). That is, even if the testimony is contrary to the interests of yourselves, your parents, or your relatives. Let not the love of your own interests or the love of parents and relatives incite you to distort or abandon it. What is meant by the testimony being "against oneself" or "against parents and relatives" is that the testimony one bears would, if delivered, be harmful to his own state or the state of his parents and relatives. This applies whether the person harmed is the one testified against directly—such as if his father and another person are in litigation and he testifies against his father—or if the harm is indirect, such as if two people are in litigation and the witness bears testimony for one of them which, if delivered, would also harm the witness himself, just as it would the other litigant.

His saying, the Exalted: **إِنْ يَكُنْ غَنِيًّا أَوْ فَقِيراً فَاللهُ أَوْلى بِهِما** "whether he be rich or poor, Allah is closer to both" (4:135). The dual pronoun (بهما) refers back to "the rich" and "the poor" despite the presence of the disjunctive "or" (أو) because what is meant by the rich and the poor is the hypothetical, unknown individual who recurs according to the occurrence of events. Thus, he may be rich in one instance and poor in another; the disjunction is according to the hypothetical explanation, while in external reality, there is multiplicity. This is how some have explained it. The meaning, then, is that Allah is closer to the rich man in his wealth and to the poor man in his poverty.

What is meant—and Allah knows best—is: let not the wealth of the rich man cause you to incline toward him away from the truth, nor the poverty of the poor man cause you to favor his condition by deviating from the truth. Rather, uphold the testimony for Allah, the Glorified, and then leave the matter between Him and the rich and the poor. He is closer to them both and more merciful toward their conditions. It is from His mercy that He made the truth that which must be followed and made justice (القسط) that which is enjoined to be upheld. In the upholding of justice and the manifestation of truth lies the felicity of the human species, by which the backbone of the rich is strengthened and the condition of the poor is rectified.

Even if one of them might benefit from a distorted or abandoned testimony in a specific case or cases, this will inevitably weaken the truth and kill justice. In that lies the strength of falsehood and the life of tyranny and injustice, which is the incurable disease and the destruction of humanity.

His saying, the Exalted: **فَلا تَتَّبِعُوا الْهَوى أَنْ تَعْدِلُوا** "so do not follow [your] desires, lest you should swerve" (4:135). That is, for fear that you might deviate from the truth and justice by following whims and abandoning testimony for Allah. Thus, His saying **أَنْ تَعْدِلُوا** "lest you should swerve" is an object of purpose (مفعول لأجله). It is also possible that it is governed by an elided preposition, relating to "following," meaning: "so that you swerve."

His saying, the Exalted: **وَإِنْ تَلْوُوا أَوْ تُعْرِضُوا فَإِنَّ اللهَ كانَ بِما تَعْمَلُونَ خَبِيراً** "and if you twist [your tongues] or disregard [the truth], then Allah is indeed aware of what you do" (4:135). "Twisting" (اللي) regarding testimony is a metaphor for distorting it, derived from "twisting the tongue" (لي اللسان). "Disregarding" (الإعراض) is to abandon the testimony altogether.

It has also been recited as **وَإِنْ تَلُوا** (with a *damma* on the *lam* and a silent *waw*), derived from "guardianship" or "authority" (الولاية). The meaning would then be: "If you take charge of the matter of testimony and deliver it, or if you disregard it, then Allah is aware of your deeds and will recompense you for them."

## (Narrative Discussion)

In the *Tafsir* of al-Qummi, it is narrated that Abu Abdillah (peace be upon him) said: "Indeed, a believer has seven rights over another believer. The most obligatory of them is that a man speaks the truth—even if it be against himself or against his parents—and does not incline away from the truth for their sake." Then he said: **فَلا تَتَّبِعُوا الْهَوى أَنْ تَعْدِلُوا وَإِنْ تَلْوُوا أَوْ تُعْرِضُوا** "so do not follow [your] desires, lest you should swerve; and if you twist [your tongues] or disregard [the truth]"—meaning, from the truth.

I say: In this, there is a generalization of the meaning of "testimony" to include speaking the truth absolutely, based on the generality of His saying: **كُونُوا قَوَّامِينَ بِالْقِسْطِ** "Be maintainers of justice."

In *al-Majma'*, it is said: It is said that the meaning of "if you twist" (تلووا) is: if you alter the testimony; and "or disregard" (أو تعرضوا) is: if you conceal it. He said: This is what is narrated from Abu Ja'far (peace be upon him).
